name: What makes loans differ
reg: The five axes that tell one mortgage from another

items.text: Every mortgage looks unique, but the differences come down to a handful of axes. Once you know the axes, any loan is easy to place. (The products themselves are taught in Module 5.)

The first axis is how the interest rate behaves. A fixed-rate mortgage locks the rate for the whole term — the principal-and-interest payment is the same in year one and year thirty. An adjustable-rate mortgage (ARM) ties the rate to a market index, so it can move, and the payment moves with it. A loan whose rate never changes is fixed; one whose rate can move is adjustable.

The next axis is whether a government agency insures or guarantees the loan. A conventional loan is not insured or guaranteed by any government agency — the lender and the secondary market carry the risk. A government loan is insured or guaranteed by a federal agency: the FHA, the VA, or the USDA. A separate axis is size. A conforming loan meets Fannie Mae and Freddie Mac standards, including a maximum dollar amount called the conforming loan limit; a jumbo loan exceeds that limit, so those two buyers won't purchase it. Two different questions, easy to conflate: conforming-versus-jumbo is about the loan amount, conventional-versus-government is about who insures or guarantees it.

Two axes remain. Lien position is the order of repayment if the property is sold or foreclosed: a first lien gets paid before anything else; a second lien — like a home-equity loan — sits behind it and is riskier for the lender. The last axis is purpose. A purchase loan buys a home the borrower doesn't yet own; a refinance replaces a mortgage on a home they already have — purchase versus refinance.
